cause: the spec-judge failed all three lenses on round 1, and the sharpest finding was that the recurrence this CR exists to prevent had happened inside the CR itself. The method was to read the upstream spec rather than this repo's restatement of it; that was done for the two principles under repair and NOT done for the two next to them, so the contract's content-first rule was found restated into a claim about command groups the source never makes, and its help rule found with half its requirement silently dropped. The bare-worktree carve-out, this CR's one deliberate exception, was justified by the invented sentence. Retracted the widening, rebuilt bare send on the principle that actually decides it, and re-grounded the carve-out on scope.

the false-tense class survived by changing tense. A prior CR here was caught twice claiming work was BUILT on a spec-only round; this conductor caught himself repeating it, rewrote to contract voice, and then asserted four times that questions were FILED when nothing was filed anywhere and the ledger held one line. One of those claims was baked into a frozen comment. A sweep keyed to the earlier wording could not catch it. Fixed by making the record real first — the follow-ups are now written to the durable ledger, which needs no permission and cannot be denied — and by demoting every claim to what is actually true at this moment.

the consumer node contradicted the suite the CR was freezing, in three places, one of them a line this CR had edited. The line was touched to correct an amendment-versus-application label and left the reversed stream and code standing beside the fix — a line-level edit that read for one claim and not the others. A fourth contradiction sat in the append-only decision log, which was never in the touch set at all and still recorded the reversed shape as a ratified decision with an accepted cost.

two new scenarios could not do their job. One fixed a world that cannot exist — it separated two subcommands by a flag, and those two subcommands define identical flag sets, so no conforming or non-conforming implementation could be told apart by it. The other asserted only that a stable code exists, which the cheapest wrong implementation at the exact helper this pass touches would satisfy: renaming free text to a single catch-all code and moving it to stdout passes every row while leaving a caller as unable to distinguish failures as parsing prose left them. Rebuilt the first on a pair that genuinely differs and the second as a table of distinct codes plus a scenario pinning distinctness itself.

the stream decision's load-bearing premise — that a verb writes its result or its error and never both — was verified true of today's code and left uncontracted, so a future streaming verb could break the CR's central safety argument with no frozen scenario noticing. It is now pinned, including the passthrough verb where a mixture would be genuinely unparseable rather than merely untidy.

round 2 found a genuine Conflict the round-1 fixes had not reached: one validator is contracted at two different exit codes by two suites, because the new layout-save usage-error row said exit 2 while frozen layout.feature pinned the same isValidLayoutName failure at exit 1. Not resolvable by editing prose — the suites disagree on behavior. Escalated to the requester, who ratified extending Clearance to the layout suite. Reclassified the four genuine usage errors there to 2 (malformed name, mutually exclusive flags, missing required parameter) and left the eighteen predicate answers and operation failures at 1, matching the same 1-versus-2 line drawn everywhere else in this CR. Also swept the corpus and fixed the one surviving consumer contradiction, layout README's save-refusals row, which still said each refusal exits 1.

round 2 caught the round-1 spot-fix pattern recurring: the unconstructible subcommand-flag scenario was rebuilt in the suite but its disproven justification was left standing in the consumer prose, which still cited two subcommands with identical flag sets as if they differed. Rebuilt the prose on the layout pair that genuinely differs and named the send pair as the counter-example it is. Also made two of my own additions honest: the surface exit-code table no longer owns layout domain specifics (it demonstrates the shape on non-layout verbs and points at the layout suite for the rest, per the boundary rule), and the result-or-error-never-both invariant was rewritten from an unbindable any-verb form to the precise one-payload-on-stdout rule, with the partial-apply manifest pinned as the case that looks like it breaks the rule and does not.

note: Re-gated on the merged tree. The target advanced by two commits (--env on every verb that opens a pane, CR-19) between the impl gate and the push, touching cli.ts, mux.feature and spec.md — the same files this CR rewrites. Rebased onto the new tip; resolved a real cli.ts conflict as deliver code work (import union; the open and worktree-open actions had to carry BOTH the incoming env field and this CR's guarded()/reportWorktreeFailure error surface) and a spec.md approval conflict (latest CR wins the block, per the standing model CR-19 itself recorded). The merge was NOT treated as lucky: full pnpm verify re-run green on the merged tree (7/7, 587 tests spanning both the --env and the error-surface suites), and the real binary driven on the exact merged error paths — open --env and worktree open --env both carry env and route their failures through the coded stdout surface. spec.md fixed a stale sendCommand docstring the incoming branch left describing the old stderr/exit-1 behavior.
